Collection › Song Dynasty › ro-song-016 — Jian Ware Tea Bowl with Hare’s Fur Glaze

Reference Object ro-song-016

Jian Ware Tea Bowl with Hare’s Fur Glaze

建窑 兔毫盏 — Song Dynasty, 12th–13th century CE — Jianyang, Fujian Province

Song Dynasty Jian Ware Tenmoku Hare’s Fur 兔毫 Iron Crystallisation ε-Fe₂O₃ Seal Mark GOM Examined

Object Description

A tea bowl of the classic Song Dynasty Jian ware form: truncated conical profile with a slightly inward-curving rim, wide mouth, and compressed foot. The proportions correspond closely to excavated examples from the Jianyang kilns in Fujian Province. The bowl carries a deep multilayer iron-saturated glaze showing the well-developed Hare’s Fur (兔毫, Tuhao) crystallisation pattern throughout the interior and upper exterior.

The glaze exhibits a characteristic colour gradient from deep iron-black at the interior and upper body to reddish-brown near the unglazed foot, produced by differential iron oxide behaviour during cooling. The interior shows well-developed elongated Hare’s Fur streaks radiating from the centre, with blue-black crystalline cores and amber peripheral zones. The unglazed foot ring exposes the dense reddish-brown iron-rich Fujian stoneware body. A seal mark is impressed on the base interior.

Microscopic Diagnostic Analysis

The bowl was examined using Gemmological Optical Microscopy (GOM) at magnifications from 20× to 100× under reflected and oblique illumination. The examination documents the Hare’s Fur crystallisation morphology, glaze stratification, bubble population, crystal aggregate structure, glaze–body interface, and clay body fabric. Observations were compared with published archaeometric investigations of authenticated Song Dynasty Jian ware using SEM, XRD, Raman spectroscopy, and XRF, particularly studies of ε-Fe₂O₃ crystallisation.

The Hare’s Fur pattern is produced by iron oxide phase separation and ε-Fe₂O₃ crystallisation during controlled cooling of the iron-saturated glaze at 1280–1320°C. This crystalline phase is metastable and forms only within a narrow temperature window during slow cooling in a reducing kiln atmosphere. It cannot be reproduced by surface application after firing. Published XRD and Raman studies confirm ε-Fe₂O₃ as the principal crystalline phase in authentic Jian ware Hare’s Fur streaks.

GOM Documentation — Glaze–Body Interface and Glaze Stratification

~50×

Fig. 5. Glaze–body interface at the foot rim. The deep iron-black multilayer glaze (left) terminates sharply against the rough iron-rich Fujian stoneware body (right). The glaze skirt morphology and absence of any secondary coating or restoration material are clearly visible. Crystal growth structures are present within the glaze layer.

~80×

Fig. 6. Bubble population within the glaze matrix. Abundant sub-surface bubbles with irregular size distribution and spherical morphology, produced by gas entrapment during high-temperature reduction firing. Iron oxide aggregates and Hare’s Fur streak cross-sections are visible in the surrounding glaze.

GOM Documentation — Crystal Aggregate Microstructure

~100×

Fig. 7. Crystal aggregate microstructure at high magnification. Needle-like crystals with radial growth pattern and iron oxide aggregation are visible within the glaze matrix. This optical morphology corresponds to published SEM observations of ε-Fe₂O₃ crystallisation in authenticated Song Dynasty Jian ware.

1. Hare’s Fur Crystallisation — 兔毫 (Tuhao)

The Hare’s Fur pattern presents the classical tripartite streak morphology: deep blue-black crystalline cores (ε-Fe₂O₃ phase) aligned radially from the bowl centre, amber-golden peripheral transition zones representing the iron-depleted outer crystallisation boundary, and natural integration with the surrounding iron-black glaze matrix. The streaks are fully encapsulated beneath the glaze surface, confirming sub-glaze crystallisation during the original firing event. This internal encapsulation is the primary diagnostic criterion distinguishing authentic Hare’s Fur from surface-painted imitations.

2. Multilayer Glaze Stratification

The deep iron-black glaze exhibits significant optical depth consistent with multiple glaze applications. Under GOM the glaze shows internal transparency, thickness-dependent colour variation, and distinct layering visible at the glaze–body interface. The colour gradient from deep iron-black at the interior and upper body to reddish-brown at the lower exterior reflects differential iron oxide behaviour: at lower temperatures during cooling, partial re-oxidation at the glaze surface produces the amber-reddish tone characteristic of authentic Jian ware.

3. Bubble Population

Microscopy reveals an abundant sub-surface bubble population with irregular size distribution and spherical morphology. These bubbles were produced by gas entrapment during high-temperature reduction firing at 1280–1320°C and are characteristic of authentic Jian kiln firing conditions. Their presence within the glaze matrix, rather than on the surface, confirms genuine formation during the original firing rather than post-firing introduction.

4. Crystal Aggregates (ε-Fe₂O₃)

High-magnification GOM reveals needle-like crystal structures with radial growth patterns corresponding to published SEM observations of ε-Fe₂O₃ crystallisation in authenticated Song Dynasty Jian ware. This metastable iron oxide phase forms only within a narrow temperature window during slow cooling in a reducing kiln atmosphere and cannot be reproduced by low-temperature or modern firing schedules. Its presence provides one of the strongest available microscopic authentication indicators for Jian ware.

5. Clay Body and Foot Construction

The unglazed foot ring exposes a dense reddish-brown iron-rich stoneware body containing quartz grains, feldspar, and iron nodules in a heterogeneous matrix characteristic of Fujian province clays used at the Jianyang kilns. The foot ring is roughly trimmed by hand with visible tool marks and kiln oxidation deposits. The natural glaze skirt terminates irregularly above the foot ring without secondary modification, and no restoration material is present.

6. Seal Mark

An impressed seal mark is visible on the base interior. The mark is integrated within the clay body surface and shows the same oxidation and ageing as the surrounding body fabric, confirming it was applied before firing. Impressed marks on Jian ware bases are documented in excavated kiln material from Jianyang and are associated with specific production periods within the Song Dynasty.

Diagnostic Interpretation

The combination of optical characteristics documented by GOM corresponds closely with published archaeometric investigations of authenticated Song Dynasty Jian ware. The convergence of Hare’s Fur crystallisation morphology, glaze stratification, bubble population, ε-Fe₂O₃ crystal aggregates, authentic clay body fabric, and natural glaze skirt morphology supports attribution to Song Dynasty Jian kiln production at Jianyang, Fujian.

Key Diagnostic Markers

Hare’s Fur (兔毫) crystallisation — tripartite streak morphology (blue-black core / amber transition / glaze matrix)
Sub-glaze encapsulation of streaks — original firing confirmed
ε-Fe₂O₃ crystal aggregates with needle-like radial morphology
Deep multilayer iron-black glaze with optical depth
Colour gradient from iron-black to reddish-amber (differential oxidation)
Abundant sub-surface bubble population (spherical, irregular distribution)
Dense iron-rich Fujian clay body (quartz, feldspar, iron nodules)
Roughly trimmed foot ring with hand-tool marks
Natural glaze skirt with sharp irregular termination
Impressed seal mark integrated in pre-firing body
Kiln oxidation deposits on foot surface
No restoration detected

References

Published XRD and Raman spectroscopy studies confirming ε-Fe₂O₃ as the principal crystalline phase in authentic Song Dynasty Jian ware Hare’s Fur streaks were consulted for comparative calibration of GOM observations.

Published SEM investigations of Jian ware glaze microstructure including bubble population, multilayer glaze stratification, and crystal aggregate morphology were used as comparative reference.

Jianyang kiln excavation reports, Fujian Province — impressed seal marks, clay body composition, and firing evidence from authenticated kiln material.
